title: NOC 65202 – Meat cutters and fishmongers – retail and wholesale | GTR Immigration
description: Meat cutters and fishmongers under NOC 65202 are the skilled technicians behind Canada’s fresh food supply chain. Operating in retail grocery environments and wholesale markets, these TEER 5 professionals specialize in the precise preparation, portioning, and presentation of meat and seafood products. As food security and quality remain top priorities for Canadian consumers in 2026, this role offers a resilient and accessible pathway for international workers. For immigration purposes, this occupation is frequently targeted by regional pilots and Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) that support the food services and retail sectors.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group652 – Support occupations in food, accommodation and tourismMinor Group6520 – Food support occupationsNOC Code65202

duties: Grind meats and slice cooked meats using powered grinders and slicing machines.
Cut, weigh, package, label, and price meat, poultry, fish, and seafood for retail sale.
Suggest complementary or alternative meat/fish products to customers and provide cooking advice.
Inspect the quality of products, check expiry dates, and ensure proper stock rotation (FIFO).
Maintain equipment, display cases, and work areas in accordance with strict hygiene standards.
Receive and store merchandise in accordance with company and safety standards.
Comply with government health and safety regulations at all times.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school may be required by some employers.
On-the-job training in food stores or wholesalers is almost always provided.
An apprenticeship program in meat cutting is available and encouraged in some provinces (e.g., BC and Saskatchewan).
Trade Certification is available but voluntary in British Columbia and Saskatchewan.
Proficiency in English or French is required for customer interaction and safety compliance.
job_outlook: The outlook for this occupation is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in job openings driven by population growth and the retirement of the aging workforce in the retail trade sector. As grocery retailers focus more on "fresh-prep" stations, the need for skilled hands-on cutters will continue to grow.
labour_market_demand: The demand for meat cutters and fishmongers is currently rated as Good to Very Good across Canada. In 2026, there is a noted labor shortage in major urban centers like Toronto and Vancouver, where the expansion of specialized grocery chains and premium seafood markets has outpaced the local supply of trained cutters. The industry is currently seeking international talent to fill essential gaps in retail food distribution.
Essential Industry Stability: Work in a sector that is recession-proof and vital to Canada’s daily food infrastructure.
On-the-Job Skill Development: Many employers offer specialized training in traditional and modern cutting techniques.
Path to Specialization: Use this experience to transition into high-TEER roles such as Butcher - retail and wholesale (NOC 63201).
PR Accessibility: A preferred occupation for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and various Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP) communities.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A direct route for workers with job offers from designated employers in the Maritimes.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A pathway for those working in the food and retail sector for 9 consecutive months in BC.
Saskatchewan INP (Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot): Designed for high-demand roles in the service and retail sectors.
Agri-Food Pilot: While primarily for industrial roles, experience in meat preparation can often be leveraged for specialized PR streams.
